pages.title: EXPOSÉ XV FROBENIUS MORPHISM AND RATIONALITY OF L FUNCTIONS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XV
FROBENIUS MORPHISM AND RATIONALITY OF L FUNCTIONSby C. HOUZEL
§ 1. FROBENIUS MORPHISM
n° 1. Frobenius endomorphism.

blocks.title: Definition 1.
blocks.html:
Definition 1.

Let \(p\) be a prime number; we say that a prescheme \(X\) is of characteristic \(p\) if \(p\cdot1_{\mathcal O_X}=0\).

This condition is equivalent to the following: the (unique) morphism \(X\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\) factors through \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_p)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb Z)\); note moreover that such a factorization is unique.

We shall denote by \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\) the full subcategory of the category of preschemes whose objects are the preschemes of characteristic \(p\). By the preceding remark, the category \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\) is naturally isomorphic to the category of \(\mathbb F_p\)-preschemes.

If \(X\) is a prescheme of characteristic \(p\), the pair \(\operatorname{fr}_X=(\operatorname{id}_{|X|},\varphi)\), where \(\varphi:\mathcal O_X\longrightarrow\mathcal O_X\) is defined by \(\varphi(f)=f^p\) for every section \(f\) of \(\mathcal O_X\) over an open set, is an endomorphism of \(X\), since \(\varphi\) is visibly an endomorphism of the sheaf of rings \(\mathcal O_X\).

blocks.title: Definition 2.
blocks.html:
Definition 2.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\); we call the Frobenius endomorphism of \(X\) the endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) thus defined.

The Frobenius endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) depends functorially on the prescheme \(X\) of characteristic \(p\), that is to say, for every morphism \(g:X\longrightarrow Y\) of preschemes of characteristic \(p\), the following diagram is commutative:
\[ \xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^{\operatorname{fr}_X}\ar[d]_g&X\ar[d]^g\\ Y\ar[r]_{\operatorname{fr}_Y}&Y. } \]
In other words, we have defined an endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}\) of the identity functor \(\operatorname{id}_{(\mathrm{Sch})_p}\).
n° 2. Relative Frobenius morphism.

Let \(S\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\); all \(S\)-preschemes are also of characteristic \(p\). If \(X\) is an \(S\)-prescheme, one sees (cf. the diagram) that \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) factors through the projection \[ \pi_{X/S}:X\mathop{\times}_S(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)\longrightarrow X, \]

where \((S,\operatorname{fr}_S)\) denotes \(S\) considered as an \(S\)-prescheme by means of the morphism \(\operatorname{fr}_S\). We shall denote the fiber product \(X\mathop{\times}_S(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)\) by \(X^{(p/S)}\), or even by \(X^{(p)}\) if no confusion results; the unique \(S\)-morphism from \(X\) to \(X^{(p/S)}\) which composed with \(\pi_{X/S}\) gives \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) is denoted by \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\); thus we obtain a commutative diagram in which the square is cartesian:
\[ \xymatrix{ &X^{(p)}\ar[dl]_{\pi_{X/S}}\ar[d]^{g^{(p)}}&X\ar[l]_{\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}}\ar@/^1.5pc/[ll]^{\operatorname{fr}_X}\ar[dl]^g\\ X\ar[d]_g&S&\\ S&S\ar[l]^{\operatorname{fr}_S} } \]
It is clear that \(X^{(p/S)}\) depends functorially on the \(S\)-prescheme \(X\); the functor
\[ P_S:X\rightsquigarrow X^{(p/S)} \]

is simply the change of base by means of the morphism \(\operatorname{fr}_S:S\longrightarrow S\). Like every change of base, it commutes with projective limits and with sums, and preserves properties such as: of finite type, separated, affine, projective, flat, étale, etc. The commutation with projective limits shows that if \(X\) is endowed with an algebraic structure (for example: group, algebra, etc.), \(X^{(p/S)}\) is naturally endowed with an algebraic structure of the same species.

Using the functoriality of \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) with respect to \(X\), one sees that the morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) depends functorially on the \(S\)-prescheme \(X\), so that we have defined a functorial morphism
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}:\operatorname{id}_{(\mathrm{Sch})/S}\rightsquigarrow P_S. \]

blocks.title: Definition 3.
blocks.html:
Definition 3.

Let \(S\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(X\) an \(S\)-prescheme. We say that the \(S\)-morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}:X\longrightarrow X^{(p/S)}\) is the Frobenius morphism of \(X\) relative to \(S\).
The first formal properties of the Frobenius morphism are stated in the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition 1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.

a) (transitivity) Let \(S\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(g:X\longrightarrow Y\) an \(S\)-morphism of \(S\)-preschemes. The following diagram is commutative and its three squares are cartesian:

\[ \xymatrix{ X\ar[r]_{\pi_{X/S}}\ar[d]_g&X^{(p/S)}\ar[r]\ar[d]^{g^{(p/S)}}&X^{(p/Y)}\ar[r]_{\operatorname{Fr}_{X/Y}}\ar[d]^{g^{(p/Y)}}&X\ar@/^1.5pc/[lll]^{\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}}\ar[dl]^g\\ Y\ar[r]_{\pi_{Y/S}}\ar[d]&Y^{(p/S)}\ar[r]_{\operatorname{Fr}_{Y/S}}\ar[d]&Y\ar[d]&\\ S&S\ar[l]^{\operatorname{fr}_S}& } \]

b) (change of base) Let \(g:S'\longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of preschemes of characteristic \(p\) and \(X\) an \(S\)-prescheme. We have a canonical isomorphism:

\[ X^{(p/S)}\mathop{\times}_S S'\xrightarrow{\sim}(X\mathop{\times}_S S')^{(p/S')} \]

functorial in \(X\), say \((X^{(p/S)})'\xrightarrow{\sim}X'^{(p/S')}\), marking with a ( {}' ) the result of the change of base by \(g\); moreover \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X'/S'}=(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S})'\) modulo the identification permitted by the preceding isomorphism.
The proof is immediate.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2. Let \(S\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(X\) an \(S\)-prescheme.

a) The relative Frobenius morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}:X\longrightarrow X^{(p/S)}\) is integral, surjective and radicial (hence it is a universal homeomorphism).

b) If \(X\longrightarrow S\) is an open immersion, \(X^{(p)}=X\) and \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}=\operatorname{id}_X\). If \(X\longrightarrow S\) is a closed immersion, identifying \(X\) with a closed ringed subspace \((|X|,\mathcal O_S/\mathcal J)\) of \(S\), then

\[ X^{(p)}=(|X|,\mathcal O_S/\mathcal J^p) \quad\text{and}\quad \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}=(\operatorname{id}_{|X|},\varphi), \]

where \(\varphi:\mathcal O_S/\mathcal J^p\longrightarrow \mathcal O_S/\mathcal J\) is the obvious augmentation.

c) Suppose \(X\) locally of finite presentation over \(S\).

1) The following three conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(X\) is unramified over \(S\),

(ii) \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is unramified,

(iii) \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is a monomorphism.

2) Similarly the following three conditions are equivalent:

\(i′\) \(X\) is étale over \(S\),

(ii′) \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is étale,

(iii′) \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is an isomorphism.

Let us prove a); the composite \(\pi_{X/S}\circ\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}=\operatorname{fr}_X\) is integral, surjective and radicial in an evident way; it follows that \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is radicial (EGA I 3.5.6 (ii)); moreover \(\pi_{X/S}\) is separated and radicial, since it is deduced from \(\operatorname{fr}_S\) by the change of base \(X\longrightarrow S\); it follows that \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is integral (EGA II 6.1.5 \(v\)) and surjective.
The proof of b) is evident, observing that
\[ \mathcal J\mathcal O_{(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)}=\mathcal J^p \quad\text{for every ideal }\mathcal J\text{ of }\mathcal O_S. \]
Proof of c): we know that for a morphism to be a mono-

morphism (resp. an isomorphism) it is necessary and sufficient that it be radicial and unramified (resp. radicial, étale and surjective); whence the equivalence of (ii) and (iii) (resp. (ii′) and (iii′)). It remains to prove that (i) is equivalent to (ii) and \(i′\) to (ii′).
For this we use the differential criterion of non-ramification (S.G.A. I 3) and the isomorphism
\[ \Omega^1_{X/S}\xrightarrow{\sim}\Omega^1_{X/X^{(p)}} \]
given by the canonical exact sequence
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^*\bigl(\Omega^1_{X^{(p)}/S}\bigr) \longrightarrow\Omega^1_{X/S}\longrightarrow \Omega^1_{X/X^{(p)}}\longrightarrow0\quad\text{(S.G.A. II 4)}, \]

in which the first arrow is visibly zero, since \(\Omega^1_{X^{(p)}/S}\) is generated by the differentials of the sections of \(\mathcal O_{X^{(p)}}\), and the differential \(d_{X/S}\) vanishes on \((\mathcal O_X)^p\) and on \(\mathcal O_S\). Thus (i) is equivalent to (ii).

Suppose now \(X\) étale over \(S\); the same is true of \(X^{(p)}\) (change of base), and consequently the morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}:X\longrightarrow X^{(p)}\) is étale. Conversely, suppose \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) étale; it is unramified, hence \(X\) is unramified over \(S\); in other words the structural morphism \(g:X\longrightarrow S\) is locally on \(X\) (for the étale topology) a closed immersion (S.G.A. I cor. 7.8). We may therefore suppose that \(g\) is a closed immersion of finite presentation, and it is a question of seeing that if \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is an isomorphism, \(g\) is an open immersion. Now \(X\) is defined by an Ideal \(\mathcal J\) of finite type:
\[ X=(|X|,\mathcal O_S/\mathcal J)\quad\text{and, by b),}\quad X^{(p)}=(|X|,\mathcal O_S/\mathcal J^p), \]

\(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}=(\operatorname{id}_{|X|}, \mathcal O_S/\mathcal J^p\longrightarrow\mathcal O_S/\mathcal J)\); to say that \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) is an isomorphism thus amounts to saying that \(\mathcal J=\mathcal J^p\), and since \(\mathcal J\) is of finite type we deduce by Nakayama's lemma that \(\mathcal J_s=0\), or \(\mathcal J_s=\mathcal O_{S,s}\) for every point \(s\in S\), whence the conclusion.
n° 3. Effect on a Module. Examples.
Let \(S\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(X\) an \(S\)-prescheme; for every \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module \(\mathcal E\) (sheaf for the Zariski topology), we set

\[ \mathcal E^{(p/S)}=\pi_{X/S}^*(\mathcal E) =\mathcal E\mathbin{\mathop\otimes_{\mathcal O_X}}\mathcal O_{X^{(p)}} =\mathcal E\mathbin{\mathop\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}}\mathcal O_{(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)}. \]
This is a \(\mathcal O_{X^{(p)}}\)-Module; we have
\[ \mathcal E^{(p/X)}\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^*(\mathcal E^{(p/S)}). \]
The most interesting case is that in which \(X=S\); we can then write \(\mathcal E^{(p)}\) instead of \(\mathcal E^{(p/S)}\) without risk of confusion;
\[ \mathcal E^{(p)}=\operatorname{fr}_S^*(\mathcal E). \]

The adjunction morphism \(\mathcal E\longrightarrow\operatorname{fr}_{S*}\operatorname{fr}_S^*(\mathcal E)\) is a morphism of abelian sheaves \(\mathcal E\longrightarrow\mathcal E^{(p)}\) which is not \(\mathcal O_S\)-linear, but "p-linear" (in an obvious sense); it is visibly a universal p-linear morphism from \(\mathcal E\) to a \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module.

It is clear that \(\mathcal E^{(p)}\) depends functorially on \(\mathcal E\) and that the functor \(\mathcal E\rightsquigarrow\mathcal E^{(p)}\) is right exact (but not exact in general); it commutes with the tensor product:
\[ (\mathcal E\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}\mathcal F)^{(p)} \xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal E^{(p)}\otimes_{\mathcal O_{X^{(p)}}}\mathcal F^{(p)}, \]

and preserves properties such as: quasi-coherent, of finite type, of finite presentation, flat, locally free, etc. In particular if \(\mathcal L\) is an invertible sheaf on \(S\), one sees immediately that \(\mathcal L^{(p)}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathcal L^{\otimes p}\), since the morphism \(\mathcal L\longrightarrow\mathcal L^{\otimes p}\) defined by raising sections to the p-th tensor power is a universal p-linear morphism.

If \(\mathcal A\) is a \(\mathcal O_S\)-Algebra, \(\mathcal A^{(p)}\) possesses a structure of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Algebra deduced from that of \(\mathcal A\). Suppose \(\mathcal A\) quasi-coherent (resp. graded and quasi-coherent); the same is true of \(\mathcal A^{(p)}\) and we have:

\[ \operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A^{(p)}) =\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}\mathcal O_{(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)}) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)\mathop\times_S (S,\operatorname{fr}_S)=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)^{(p)} \]
(resp.

\[ \operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A^{(p)}) =\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}\mathcal O_{(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)}) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A)\mathop\times_S (S,\operatorname{fr}_S)=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A)^{(p)}). \]

The projection \(\pi_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)/S}: \operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)^{(p)}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)\) (resp. \(\pi_{\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A)/S}\)) is identified with the morphism deduced from \(\mathcal A\longrightarrow\mathcal A^{(p)}\). On the other hand \(\operatorname{Fr}_{\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)/S}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Fr}_{\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A)/S}\)) comes from a homomorphism of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Algebras
\[ \mathcal A^{(p)}=\mathcal A\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}\mathcal O_{(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)} \longrightarrow\mathcal A \]

which transforms \(a\otimes f\) into \(a^pf\) for every section \(a\) of \(\mathcal A\) and every section \(f\) of \(\mathcal O_S\). To see this one observes that the composite of this homomorphism with \(\mathcal A\longrightarrow\mathcal A^{(p)}\) (on the right) is the raising to the p-th power in \(\mathcal A\).

Consider the particular case where \(\mathcal A\) is the symmetric Algebra \(\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E)\) of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(\mathcal E\); it is clear that \(\mathcal A^{(p)}\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E^{(p)})\) hence

\[ \mathbf V(\mathcal E^{(p)})\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A^{(p)}) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)^{(p)}=\mathbf V(\mathcal E)^{(p)}, \] \[ \mathbf P(\mathcal E^{(p)})\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A^{(p)}) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A)^{(p)}=\mathbf P(\mathcal E)^{(p)} ; \]
as for \(\operatorname{Fr}_{\mathbf V(\mathcal E)/S}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Fr}_{\mathbf P(\mathcal E)/S}\)), it factors in the following way:

\[ \mathbf V(\mathcal E)\longrightarrow\mathbf V(\operatorname{Sym}^p(\mathcal E)) \longrightarrow\mathbf V(\mathcal E^{(p)}) \quad(\text{resp. }\mathbf P(\mathcal E)\longrightarrow \mathbf P(\operatorname{Sym}^p(\mathcal E))\longrightarrow\mathbf P(\mathcal E^{(p)})) \]
where the first arrow is defined by the canonical morphism:
\[ \operatorname{Sym}(\operatorname{Sym}^p(\mathcal E)) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E) \]
which comes from \(\operatorname{Sym}^p(\mathcal E)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E)\) while the second is defined by the morphism of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules
\[ \mathcal E^{(p)}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sym}^p(\mathcal E) \]

coming from the p-linear morphism: \(\mathcal E\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sym}^p(\mathcal E)\), given by raising sections to the p-th symmetric power. In the case of \(X=\mathbf P(\mathcal E)\), let us compute the fundamental sheaf \(\mathcal O_{X^{(p)}}(1)\):
\[ \mathcal O_{X^{(p)}}(1) =(\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E)^{(p)}(1))^{\sim} =(\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E)(1)^{(p)})^{\sim} \xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal O_X(1)^{(p/S)}. \]

When \(\mathcal E=\mathcal O_S^n\), \(\operatorname{Sym}(\mathcal E)=\mathcal O_S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) and \(X=\mathbf V(\mathcal E)=\mathbb S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) (resp. \(X=\mathbf P(\mathcal E)=\mathbb P_S^{n-1}\)); it is clear that \(\mathcal E^{(p)}\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal O_S^n=\mathcal E\), so that \(X^{(p)}=X\). The Frobenius morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}\) comes from the endomorphism of the Algebra \(\mathcal O_S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) which transforms \(T_i\) into \(T_i^p\) \((i=1,\ldots,n)\).

Consider now a prescheme \(X\) affine of finite type (resp. projective) over \(S\), defined by an Ideal (resp. a homogeneous Ideal) \(\mathcal J\subset\mathcal O_S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\). Thus \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A)\) (resp. \(=\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A)\)) where \(\mathcal A=\mathcal O_S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]/\mathcal J\); one sees that
\[ \mathcal A^{(p)}\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal O_S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]/\mathcal I \]

where \(\mathcal I\) is the image in \(\mathcal O_S[T_1,\ldots,T_n]\) of \(\mathcal J^{(p)}=\mathcal J\otimes_{\mathcal O_S}\mathcal O_{(S,\operatorname{fr}_S)}\); the Ideal \(\mathcal I\) is deduced from \(\mathcal J\) by raising the coefficients of the polynomials to the p-th power; we have \(X^{(p)}=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal A^{(p)})\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Proj}(\mathcal A^{(p)})\)), and the Frobenius morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}:X\longrightarrow X^{(p)}\) is defined by raising the "coordinates" \(T_i\) to the p-th power (which determines a morphism \(\mathcal A^{(p)}\longrightarrow\mathcal A\)).
n° 4. Iterated Frobenius morphism; perfect preschemes.

For every integer \(\nu\geq0\), the iterated endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_X^\nu\) depends functorially on the prescheme \(X\) of characteristic \(p\). If therefore \(S\) is a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(X\) an \(S\)-prescheme, \(\operatorname{fr}_X^\nu\) factors (in a unique manner) into an \(S\)-morphism
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^\nu:X\longrightarrow X^{(p^\nu/S)}, \quad\text{where}\quad X^{(p^\nu/S)}=X\mathop\times_S(S,\operatorname{fr}_S^\nu), \]

followed by the projection: \(X^{(p^\nu/S)}\longrightarrow X\) (cf. n°2). The morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^\nu\) depends functorially on the \(S\)-prescheme \(X\) and is compatible with the change of the base \(S\) (cf. n°2, prop. 1b)). Moreover it is clear that
\[ X^{(p^\nu/S)}=X^{(p^\nu)}\xrightarrow{\sim} (X^{(p^{\nu-1})})^{(p)} \quad\text{and that}\quad \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^\nu=(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^{\nu-1})^{(p)} \circ\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S} \]
modulo the identification permitted by the preceding canonical isomorphism.

blocks.title: Definition 4.
blocks.html:
Definition 4.

We say that a prescheme \(S\) of characteristic \(p\) is perfect if \(\operatorname{fr}_S\) is an automorphism of \(S\).

If \(S\) is a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) which is perfect and \(X\) an \(S\)-prescheme, one sees immediately that \(X^{(p/S)}\) is identified with the prescheme \(X\) considered as an \(S\)-prescheme by means of the morphism \(\operatorname{fr}_S^{-1}\circ g:X\longrightarrow S\), where \(g\) is the structural morphism of \(X\) into \(S\); modulo this identification \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}=\operatorname{fr}_X\).

As an example of a perfect prescheme, we encounter \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_q)\) where \(q=p^\nu\) is a power of the characteristic \(p\); indeed in this case \(\operatorname{fr}_S^\nu=\operatorname{id}_S\). If \(X\) is an \(S\)-prescheme, one sees
\[ X^{(p^\nu/S)}=X\quad\text{and}\quad \operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}^{\nu}=\operatorname{fr}_X^\nu. \]

Note that for \(\nu=1\) we obtain \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_p)\) for which \(\operatorname{fr}_S=\operatorname{id}_S\); the \(S\)-preschemes are the preschemes of characteristic \(p\); over this base \(S\), \(X^{(p/S)}=X\) and \(\operatorname{Fr}_{X/S}=\operatorname{fr}_X\).
§ 2. FROBENIUS CORRESPONDENCE.
n° 1. Definition.

Let \(X\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\) and \(F\) a sheaf of sets on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\). For every étale \(X\)-prescheme \(U\) we have \[ ((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F))(U)=F(\operatorname{fr}_X^{-1}(U)) =F(U^{(p/X)}), \] and the Frobenius morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X}:U\longrightarrow U^{(p/X)}\) defines a map
\[ F(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X}):(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)(U)\longrightarrow F(U) \]
visibly functorially in \(U\) (since \(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X}\) is functorial in \(U\)). Thus we obtain a morphism of sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\):
\[ (\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)\longrightarrow F. \]

Now, when \(U\) is étale over \(X\), \(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X}\) is an isomorphism (§ 1 n° 2, prop. 2c)), hence the same is true of \(F(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X})\); it follows that the preceding morphism \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)\longrightarrow F\) is an isomorphism; in the case where \(F\) is endowed with an algebraic structure (for example if it is a sheaf of \(\Lambda\)-modules, \(\Lambda\) a given base ring) this isomorphism is compatible with the algebraic structure. The inverse
\[ F(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X})^{-1}:F\longrightarrow(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F) \]
of this isomorphism defines, since
\((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*\) is left adjoint to the functor \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*\), a morphism
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*:(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)\longrightarrow F. \]

One can even say more, for \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) being a universal homeomorphism (§ 1 n° 2, prop. 2a)), the functors \((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*\) and \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*\) are quasi-inverse to each other, and consequently \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) is an isomorphism, as is the morphism \(F(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X})^{-1}\) from which it comes.

blocks.title: Definition 1.
blocks.html:
Definition 1.

Given a prescheme \(X\) of characteristic \(p\) and a sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\), we call Frobenius correspondence on \((X,F)\) the pair \((\operatorname{fr}_X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)\) formed by the Frobenius endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) of \(X\) and the isomorphism \[ \operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*=(F(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X})^{-1})^\#: (\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)\longrightarrow F. \]

Of course one defines in the same way a class of iterated Frobenius correspondence \((\operatorname{fr}_X^\nu,(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)^\nu)\) for every integer \(\nu\geq0\); the isomorphism \[ (\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)^\nu:(\operatorname{fr}_X^\nu)^*(F)\longrightarrow F \] is equal to \[ (\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)^{\nu-1}\circ (\operatorname{fr}_X^{\nu-1})^*(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*) \]
or again to \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\circ\operatorname{fr}_X^* ((\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)^{\nu-1})\) for \(\nu\geq1\).

Let us examine the particular case where \(F\) is representable by a prescheme \(V\) étale over \(X\); then \((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)=V^{(p/X)}\), \[ F(V)=\operatorname{Hom}_X(V,V),\qquad (\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)(V)=F(V^{(p/X)}) =\operatorname{Hom}_X(V^{(p/X)},V) \] and \(F(\operatorname{Fr}_{V/X})\) is the map from \(\operatorname{Hom}_X(V^{(p/X)},V)\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}_X(V,V)\) which transforms a morphism \(\varphi\) into \(\varphi\circ\operatorname{Fr}_{V/X}\); now \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) is identified with a morphism from \(V^{(p/X)}\) to \(V\) which is the image of \(\operatorname{id}_V\) under the map \(F(\operatorname{Fr}_{V/X})^{-1}\) inverse to the preceding one. This shows that for \(F=V\) étale over \(X\) we have:
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{V/X}^*=(\operatorname{Fr}_{V/X})^{-1}. \]
Note that if the sheaf \(F\) is constant it is clear that \((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)=F\) and \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*=\operatorname{id}_F\).

blocks.title: Proposition 1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.

a) The isomorphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) depends functorially on the sheaf \(F\); in other words we have a functorial isomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{/X}^*:(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*\longrightarrow \operatorname{id}_{\widetilde X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}}, \]

(where \(\widetilde X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) is the category of sheaves of sets on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\)).

b) The Frobenius correspondence \((\operatorname{fr}_X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)\) on \((X,F)\) is compatible with the change of the base \(X\). That is to say, if \(\mathcal F\) denotes the fibered category over \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\) of sheaves (for the étale topology) on variable preschemes of characteristic \(p\), the functors \((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*\) \((X\in\operatorname{Ob}(\mathrm{Sch})_p)\) define a cartesian \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\)-functor \(\operatorname{fr}^*:\mathcal F\longrightarrow\mathcal F\), and the morphisms \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) define a functorial \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\)-morphism \(\operatorname{Fr}^*/:\operatorname{fr}^*\longrightarrow \operatorname{id}_{\mathcal F}\) (which is an isomorphism).

c) Conversely these properties characterize \(\operatorname{Fr}^*/\), that is, there exists one and only one functorial \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\)-morphism from \(\operatorname{fr}^*\) to \(\operatorname{id}_{\mathcal F}\).
Let us remark that assertion b) implies assertion a); the latter is completely evident.

Let us prove b). Let \(g:X'\longrightarrow X\) be a morphism of preschemes of characteristic \(p\) and \(F\) a sheaf on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\); set \(F'=g^*(F)\), and more generally mark with a \('\) the inverse image under \(g\) of any object given on \(X\). Since \(g\circ\operatorname{fr}_{X'}=\operatorname{fr}_X\circ g\), we have
\[ (\operatorname{fr}_{X'})^*(F')=(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})^*(g^*(F)) =g^*((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F))=((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F))', \]
whence the fact that the \((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*\) define a cartesian functor \(\operatorname{fr}^*\) above \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\). It remains to prove that
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*=(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)' ; \]
using the properties of adjoint functors, one sees that this amounts to showing that the image of \(F'(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X'})^{-1}\) under the bijective map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}_{X'}(F',(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})_*(F')) =\operatorname{Hom}_{X'}(g^*(F),(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})_*g^*(F)) \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}_X(F,g_*(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})_*g^*(F)) \]
(\(g^*\) adjoint of \(g_*\)) coincides with the image of \(F(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X})^{-1}\) under the map
\[ \operatorname{Hom}_X(F,(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F))\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_X(F,(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*g_*g^*(F)) =\operatorname{Hom}_X(F,g_*(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})_*g^*(F)) \]
defined by the adjunction morphism \(\alpha:F\longrightarrow g_*g^*(F)\); in other words, it must be shown that
\[ \alpha\circ F(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X})= g_*\bigl(F'(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X'})\bigr)\circ (\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(\alpha), \]
that is, that for every prescheme \(U\) étale over \(X\) we have a commutative diagram:
\[ \xymatrix{ F(U^{(p/X)})\ar[r]^{F(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X})}\ar[d]&F(U)\ar[d]\\ F'(U'^{(p/X')})\ar[r]^{F'(\operatorname{Fr}_{U'/X'})}&F'(U') .} \]
where the vertical arrows are the canonical maps; now this comes immediately from \(\operatorname{Fr}_{U'/X'}=(\operatorname{Fr}_{U/X})'\) (§ 1, n° 2, prop. 1b)).
To prove c), one reduces, by composing with \((\operatorname{Fr}^*/)^{-1}\), to proving that \(\operatorname{id}_{\mathcal F}\) has only one \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\)-endomorphism: the identity.

Now if \(\varphi\) is a \((\mathrm{Sch})_p\)-endomorphism of \(\operatorname{id}_{\mathcal F}\), let \(F\) be a sheaf on a prescheme \(X\) of characteristic \(p\) and \(U\) a prescheme étale over \(X\); we know that \(F(U)\) is identified with \(\operatorname{Hom}_U(U,F|U)\)
and \(\varphi_F(U):F(U)\longrightarrow F(U)\) with the map:
\[ s\longmapsto\varphi_{F|U}\circ s=s\circ\varphi_U \qquad(s\in\operatorname{Hom}_U(U,F|U)); \]

as the final sheaf \(U\) of \(\widetilde U_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) admits no endomorphism other than the identity, we have \(\varphi_U=\operatorname{id}_U\); it follows that \(\varphi_F(U)\) is also the identity of \(F(U)\) and that \(\varphi=\operatorname{id}_{\mathcal F}\).

Of course, if the sheaf \(F\) is endowed with an algebraic structure, the isomorphism \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) is compatible with this structure; for example if \(F\) is a sheaf of groups on the prescheme \(X\) (of characteristic \(p\)), \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of groups. Let \(\Lambda\) be a ring and let \(F\) be a sheaf of \(\Lambda\)-modules on \(X\); then \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of \(\Lambda\)-modules; more generally, if \(F^\bullet\) is a complex of \(\Lambda\)-Modules, one immediately defines an isomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{F^\bullet/X}^*:(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F^\bullet) \longrightarrow F^\bullet \]

equal to \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F^i/X}^*\) on the component of degree \(i\) (thanks to proposition 1a)); in this case the pair \((\operatorname{fr}_X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F^\bullet/X}^*)\) is indeed a correspondence (in the usual sense) on \((X,F^\bullet)\); this correspondence is functorial in \(F^\bullet\) and compatible with the change of the base \(X\) (proposition 1a), b)).
n° 2. Effect on cohomology.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2. Let \(X\) be a prescheme of characteristic \(p\).

a) For every sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\), the endomorphism of \(H^0(X,F)\) defined by \((\operatorname{fr}_X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)\), that is, the composite

\[ H^0(X,F)\longrightarrow H^0(X,(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)) \longrightarrow H^0(X,F), \]

where the first arrow is the canonical map (contravariant character of \(H^0(X,F)\) with respect to \(X\)) and the second \(H^0(X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)\), is the identity of \(H^0(X,F)\).

b) For every sheaf of groups \(F\) on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\), the endomorphism of \(H^1(X,F)\) defined by \((\operatorname{fr}_X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)\), that is,

\[ H^1(X,F)\longrightarrow H^1(X,(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)) \longrightarrow H^1(X,F), \]

is the identity.

c) For every complex \(F\) of \(\Lambda\)-Modules on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) bounded below, the endomorphism of \(R\Gamma_X(F)\) defined by \((\operatorname{fr}_X,\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*)\), that is, the composite:

\[ R\Gamma_X(F)\longrightarrow R\Gamma_X((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)) \longrightarrow R\Gamma_X(F), \]

is the identity.

Let us first establish a); we shall deduce b) and c) from it. Let \(\varphi_F\) be the endomorphism of \(H^0(X,F)\) under consideration; it is clear that \(\varphi_F\) depends functorially on \(F\) and that \(\varphi_X=\operatorname{id}_{H^0(X,X)}\). Now every element \(s\) of \(H^0(X,F)\) is identified with a morphism of sheaves
\[ s:X\longrightarrow F \]

such that \(s\) is the image under \(H^0(X,s)\) of the unique element of \(H^0(X,X)\); since \(\varphi_F\circ H^0(X,s)=H^0(X,s)\circ\varphi_X=H^0(X,s)\) by the preceding remarks, one sees that \(\varphi_F(s)=s\) for every \(s\), hence \(\varphi_F=\operatorname{id}_{H^0(X,F)}\).
Note that \(\varphi_F\) is again the composite:
\[ H^0(X,F)\longrightarrow H^0(X,(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)) \longrightarrow H^0(X,F) \]

where the first arrow is \(H^0(X,F(\operatorname{Fr}_{/X})^{-1})\) and the second the canonical map (which is none other than the identity); similarly the endomorphisms considered in b) and c) factor as follows:
\[ H^1(X,F)\longrightarrow H^1(X,(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)) \longrightarrow H^1(X,F) \]
and
\[ R\Gamma_X(F)\longrightarrow R\Gamma_X((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)) \longrightarrow R\Gamma_X(F). \]

To establish b) we shall use a monomorphism \(F\longrightarrow G\) from \(F\) into a sheaf of groups \(G\) such that \(H^1(X,G)=0\); then \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)\longrightarrow(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(G)\) is a monomorphism and \(H^1(X,(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(G))=0\). Let us introduce the quotient sheaf of homogeneous sets \(C=G/F\);

since \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) is integral, surjective and radicial, the functors \(\operatorname{fr}_X^*\) and \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*\) are equivalences of categories quasi-inverse to each other (SGAA VIII theorem 1.1); we deduce that
\[ (\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(C)\simeq (\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(G)/(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F), \]

and the exact cohomology sequence (SGAA XII proposition 3.1) shows that the endomorphism of \(H^1(X,F)\) under consideration comes by passage to the quotient from \(\varphi_C:H^0(X,C)\longrightarrow H^0(X,C)\), which is the identity by a); this endomorphism is therefore \(\operatorname{id}_{H^1(X,F)}\).

Assertion c) is proved in an analogous manner, using a complex \(I\) injective in each degree and isomorphic to \(F\) in the derived category \(D^+\), constructed on the \(\Lambda\)-Modules; the complex \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(I)\) is injective in each degree and isomorphic to \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F)\) in \(D^+\), since \((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*\) is an equivalence of categories. One can then identify \(R\Gamma_X(F)\) with \(H^0(X,I)\) and \(R\Gamma_X((\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(F))\) with \(H^0(X,(\operatorname{fr}_X)_*(I))\); one sees that the endomorphism under consideration of \(R\Gamma_X(F)\) is identified with \(\varphi_I\), which is the identity; this endomorphism is therefore \(\operatorname{id}_{R\Gamma_X(F)}\).
n° 3. Behavior of products.

Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be preschemes of characteristic \(p\); their product \(X\times X'\) is identical to the fiber product \(X\times_eX'\) where \(e=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_p)\). It is clear that

\[ \operatorname{fr}_{X\times X'}=\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{fr}_{X'} =(\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{id}_{X'})\circ (\operatorname{id}_X\times\operatorname{fr}_{X'}) =(\operatorname{id}_X\times\operatorname{fr}_{X'})\circ (\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{id}_{X'}). \]
From this one deduces immediately that for every \(X\)-prescheme \(Y\) and every \(X'\)-prescheme \(Y'\), we have a canonical isomorphism
\[ (Y\times Y')^{(p/X\times X')}\simeq Y^{(p/X)}\times Y'^{(p/X')} \]
modulo this isomorphism one
can write \(\operatorname{Fr}_{Y\times Y'/X\times X'}= \operatorname{Fr}_{Y/X}\times\operatorname{Fr}_{Y'/X'}\).

blocks.title: Proposition 3.
blocks.html:
Proposition 3. Let \(X\) and \(X'\) be preschemes of characteristic \(p\), \(\Lambda\) a commutative ring, \(F\) a sheaf of \(\Lambda\)-modules on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) and \(F'\) a sheaf of \(\Lambda\)-modules on \(X'_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\). We have
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{F\boxtimes_\Lambda F'/X\times X'}^* =\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes_\Lambda\operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^* =(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes\operatorname{id}_{(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})^*(F')}) \circ(\operatorname{id}_F\boxtimes\operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*) \] \[ =(\operatorname{id}_{(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)}\boxtimes \operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*)\circ (\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes\operatorname{id}_{F'}). \]

To write these equalities, we have identified \((\operatorname{fr}_{X\times X'})^*(F\boxtimes_\Lambda F')\) with the tensor product \((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)\boxtimes_\Lambda (\operatorname{fr}_{X'})^*(F')\) by means of the canonical isomorphism.

The two sides of the first equality depend functorially on \(F\) and on \(F'\) and they are compatible with the change of the base \(X\) or of the base \(X'\); moreover they are compatible with passage to the inductive limit on \(F\) or on \(F'\), since the tensor product commutes with the inductive limit and it is clear that
\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{(\varinjlim F_i)/X}^*=\varinjlim \operatorname{Fr}_{F_i/X}^*; \]
this allows us to reduce to the case where \(F=\Lambda_X\) and \(F'=\Lambda_{X'}\); then it is evident.

Corollary. With the data of proposition 3, the endomorphisms of \(R\Gamma_{X\times X'}(F\boxtimes F')\) defined respectively by \[ (\operatorname{id}_X\times\operatorname{fr}_{X'}, \operatorname{id}_F\boxtimes\operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*) \quad\text{and by}\quad (\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{id}_{X'}, \operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes\operatorname{id}_{F'}) \]
are isomorphisms inverse to each other.
Denoting these endomorphisms by \(\varphi'\) and by \(\varphi\), let us show for example that \(\varphi\circ\varphi'=\operatorname{id}\); this follows from the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=2.8em@R=2.8em{ R\Gamma_{X\times X'}(F\boxtimes F') \ar[r]^{\operatorname{id}_X\times\operatorname{fr}_{X'}} \ar[dr]_{\operatorname{fr}_{X\times X'}}& R\Gamma_{X\times X'}(F\boxtimes(\operatorname{fr}_{X'})^*(F')) \ar[r]^{R\Gamma(\operatorname{id}_F\boxtimes\operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*)} \ar[d]^{\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{id}_{X'}}& R\Gamma_{X\times X'}(F\boxtimes F') \ar[d]^{\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{id}_{X'}}\\ &R\Gamma_{X\times X'}((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)\boxtimes (\operatorname{fr}_{X'})^*(F')) \ar[r]^{R\Gamma(\operatorname{id}_{(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)}\boxtimes \operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*)} \ar[dr]_{R\Gamma(\operatorname{Fr}_{F\boxtimes F'/X\times X'}^*)}& R\Gamma_{X\times X'}((\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)\boxtimes F') \ar[d]^{R\Gamma(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes\operatorname{id}_{F'})}\\ &&R\Gamma_{X\times X'}(F\boxtimes F'), } \]

which is commutative because of \(\operatorname{fr}_{X\times X'}=(\operatorname{fr}_X\times \operatorname{id}_{X'})\circ(\operatorname{id}_X\times \operatorname{fr}_{X'})\) (for the upper triangle) and of

\[ \operatorname{Fr}_{F\boxtimes F'/X\times X'}^*= (\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes\operatorname{id}_{F'})\circ (\operatorname{id}_{(\operatorname{fr}_X)^*(F)}\boxtimes \operatorname{Fr}_{F'/X'}^*) \]

(for the lower triangle), and in which the composite of the two oblique arrows is the identity by proposition 2c) (n° 2). By means of an analogous diagram exchanging the roles of \((X,F)\) and \((X',F')\) we obtain: \(\varphi'\circ\varphi=\operatorname{id}\).

We shall apply this result to the particular case where \(X'=e=\operatorname{Spec}(\overline{\mathbb F}_p)\) is the spectrum of an algebraic closure \(\overline{\mathbb F}_p\) of \(\mathbb F_p\) and where \(F'=\Lambda_e\) is the constant sheaf with stalk \(\Lambda\). In this case

We shall set: \(\overline X=X\times e=X\otimes_{\mathbb F_p} \overline{\mathbb F}_p\) and \(\overline F=F\boxtimes_\Lambda\Lambda_e\) = inverse image of \(F\) under the projection \(\overline X\longrightarrow X\). The Frobenius endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_e\) is identified with the canonical generator \(f:\lambda\longmapsto\lambda^p\) \((\lambda\in\overline{\mathbb F}_p)\) of the Galois group \(\pi_e=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb F}_p/\mathbb F_p)\) (\(f\) determines an isomorphism \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}\xrightarrow{\sim}\pi_e\)), and \(\operatorname{Fr}_{\Lambda_e/e}^*=\operatorname{id}_{\Lambda_e}\) since \(\Lambda_e\) is a constant sheaf. Let us set:

\[ \overline{\operatorname{fr}}_X=\operatorname{fr}_X\times\operatorname{id}_e \quad\text{(image réciproque de \(\operatorname{fr}_X\) par \(\overline X\longrightarrow X\))}, \] \[ f_X=\operatorname{id}_X\times\operatorname{fr}_e \quad\text{(automorphism of \(\overline X\) defined by \(f\), by transport of structure)}, \] \[ \operatorname{Fr}_{\overline F/\overline X}^* =\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\boxtimes\operatorname{id}_{\Lambda_e} \quad\text{(image réciproque de \(\operatorname{Fr}_{F/X}^*\) par \(\overline X\longrightarrow X\))}. \]

We can write: \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}=\overline{\operatorname{fr}}_X\circ f_X =f_X\circ\overline{\operatorname{fr}}_X\) and \(\operatorname{Fr}_{\overline F/\overline X}^* =\operatorname{Fr}_{\overline F/X}^*\). The corollary of proposition 3 shows that the endomorphism of \(R\Gamma_{\overline X}(\overline F)\) defined by the pair \((\overline{\operatorname{fr}}_X, \operatorname{Fr}_{\overline F/\overline X}^*)\) is the automorphism inverse to the one defined by \(f_X\). We shall say that \((\overline{\operatorname{fr}}_X, \operatorname{Fr}_{\overline F/\overline X}^*)\) is the geometric Frobenius correspondence (it is the inverse image under \(\overline X\longrightarrow X\) of the Frobenius correspondence on \((X,F)\)) and we shall denote by \(\overline{\operatorname{fr}}_X R\Gamma_{\overline X}(\overline F)\) the automorphism of \(R\Gamma_{\overline X}(\overline F)\) that it defines; the arithmetic Frobenius operation, coming by transport of structure from \(f\in\pi_e\), is its inverse.
§ 3. THE L FUNCTION
n° 1. Definition.

Let \(p\) be a prime number; consider a prime number \(\ell\ne p\) and a finite extension \(\Omega\) of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\). To every pair \((X,F)\) consisting of a scheme \(X\) of finite type over \(e=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_p)\) and a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), we are going to associate a formal power series
\[ L_F\in\Omega[[t]], \]
with constant term 1.

Let us first consider the particular case where \(X=e\); let \(\overline{\mathbb F}_p\) be an algebraic closure of \(\mathbb F_p\) and let \(\overline e=\operatorname{Spec}(\overline{\mathbb F}_p)\). The sheaf \(F\) is entirely determined by its stalk \(F_{\overline e}\), considered as a finite-dimensional \(\Omega\)-vector space on which the Galois group \(\pi_e=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb F}_p/\mathbb F_p)\) acts continuously. Since \(\pi_e\) is topologically generated by the Frobenius element \(f:\lambda\longmapsto\lambda^p\) \((\lambda\in\overline{\mathbb F}_p)\), the datum of the sheaf \(F\) is equivalent to that of the vector space \(F_{\overline e}\) endowed with a "topologically unipotent" automorphism \(f_{F_{\overline e}}\), that is, one whose \(k\)-th power tends to the identity in the analytic group \(\operatorname{Aut}(F_{\overline e})\) as \(k\) tends "multiplicatively" to infinity. We shall set
\[ L_F(t)=1/\det(1-f_{F_{\overline e}}^{-1}t)\in\Omega[[t]]. \]

Suppose now that \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_q)\), with \(q=p^d\) (\(d\) an integer \(\geq1\)). Let \(\overline x\) be a geometric point of \(X\), defined by an embedding of \(\mathbb F_q\) into \(\overline{\mathbb F}_p\); the Galois group \(\pi_x=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb F}_p/\mathbb F_q)\) is identified with the subgroup of \(\pi_e\) topologically generated by \(f^d\). Thus the datum of the sheaf \(F\) is equivalent to that of its stalk \(F_{\overline x}\) (finite-dimensional vector space over \(\Omega\)) endowed with a topologically unipotent automorphism \((f^d)_{F_{\overline x}}\), which we shall denote by \(f^d_{F_{\overline x}}\) for simplicity. Let \(g\) be the unique morphism from \(X\) to \(e\); the direct image \(g_*(F)\) is likewise defined by its stalk \(g_*(F)_{\overline e}\) endowed with the operations of \(\pi_e\), which one computes as an "induced module" \(g_*(F)_{\overline e}\simeq F_{\overline x}\otimes_{\pi_x}\pi_e\). We shall set
an easy determinant computation shows that this expression is equal
to
\[ L_F(t)=1/\det(1-f_{F_{\overline x}}^{-d}t^d). \]
In the general case of a scheme \(X\) of finite type over \(e\) endowed with a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf \(F\), one defines
\[ L_F=\prod_{x\in X^o}L_{F|x}, \]

where \(X^o\) denotes the set of closed points of \(X\); we shall see that this product is multipliable in \(\Omega[[t]]\); let us make it explicit, in fact, by introducing for each \(x\in X^o\) a geometric point \(\overline x\) of \(X\) localized at \(x\), defined by an embedding of the residue field \(k(x)\) into \(\overline{\mathbb F}_p\), and setting
\[ d(x)=[k(x):\mathbb F_p]\quad\text{(degree of (x))}\ ; \]
with these notations
\[ L_{F|x}(t)=1/\det(1-f_{F_{\overline x}}^{-d(x)}t^{d(x)}) =1+\operatorname{Tr}(f_{F_{\overline x}}^{-d(x)})t^{d(x)}+\cdots . \]

Since for every integer \(n\) the set of points \(x\in X^o\) such that \(d(x)\leq n\) is finite, one concludes that the family \((L_{F|x})_{x\in X^o}\) is multipliable in \(\Omega[[t]]\). Thus the function \(L\) of \((X,F)\) is
\[ L_F(t)=\prod_{x\in X^o}\bigl(1/\det(1-f_{F_{\overline x}}^{-d(x)}t^{d(x)})\bigr). \]
The first formal properties of the function \(L\) are collected in the following statement:

blocks.title: Proposition 1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.

a) (multiplicativity in \(F\)) Let \(0\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F''\longrightarrow0\)

be an exact sequence of constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaves on \(X\); then we have \(L_F=L_{F'}\cdot L_{F''}\).

b) Let \(Y\) be a closed subscheme of \(X\) and \(U=X-Y\) the complementary open set. Then we have: \(L_F=L_{F|U}\cdot L_{F|Y}\) for every constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf \(F\).

c) Let \(X\longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of schemes of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\), and let \(F\) be a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf on \(X\); then

\[ L_F=\prod_{s\in S^o}L_{F|X_s}. \]

Assertion a) is clear; b) comes from \(X^o=U^o\cup Y^o\) (disjoint union) and assertion c) from \(X^o=\coprod_{s\in S^o}X_s^o\), using the associativity property of multipliable infinite products; these formulas about closed points are true because the image of a closed point under a morphism of preschemes of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\) is again a closed point.
n° 2. Statement of the rationality theorem and reduction to the case of a curve.

blocks.title: Theorem.
blocks.html:
Theorem.

Let \(X\) be a scheme of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\), of dimension \(n\), \(g:X\longrightarrow e\) its structural morphism, and \(F\) a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf on \(X\). We have

\[ (1)\qquad L_F=\prod_{i=0}^{2n}\bigl(L_{R^i_!g(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i}. \]

In this statement the \(R^i_!g(F)\) are the higher direct images with proper supports, in the sense of \(\ell\)-adic cohomology. We set \(H_c^i(\overline X,\overline F)=R^i_!\overline g(\overline F) =R^i_!g(F)_{\overline e}\) (base change theorem), where

\(\overline X\), \(\overline F\) and \(\overline g\) come from \(X\), \(F\) and \(g\) by the change of base \(\overline e\longrightarrow e\) (\(\overline e\) = spectrum of an algebraic closure of \(\mathbb F_p\)); with this notation, we have by n° 1:
\[ L_{R^i_!g(F)}(t)=1/\det(1-f^{-1}_{H_c^i(\overline X,\overline F)}t) =L_{f^{-1}_{H_c^i(\overline X,\overline F)}}(t) \]
setting \(L_u(t)=1/\det(1-ut)\) for every endomorphism \(u\) of a finite-dimensional vector space, according to the formalism developed in a preceding exposé.

More generally, consider a morphism \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) of schemes of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\) and a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf \(F\) on \(X\). Denote by \((P_{F,u})\) the following property:
\[ (P_{F,u}):\qquad L_F=\prod_i\bigl(L_{R^i_!u(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i}. \]

The statement of the theorem for the pair \((X,F)\) is none other than \((P_{F,g})\), where \(g:X\longrightarrow e\) is the structural morphism of \(X\). We are going to reduce it to the case where \(X\) is the projective line by means of some lemmas.

blocks.title: Lemma 1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.

Let \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of schemes of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\) and \(F\) a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf. If \((P_{F|X_y,u_y})\) is true for every \(y\in Y^o\), then \((P_{F,u})\) is true.
Indeed

\[ \begin{aligned} L_F&=\prod_{y\in Y^o}L_{F|X_y}\qquad\text{(no. 1, proposition 1 c))}\\ &=\prod_{y\in Y^o}\prod_i\bigl(L_{R^i_!u_y(F|X_y)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i} \quad\text{using }(P_{F|X_y,u_y})\ ; \end{aligned} \]
now \(R^i_!u_y(F|X_y)\simeq R^i_!u(F)|_y\) (base change theorem), which
gives:
\[ L_F=\prod_i\left(\prod_{y\in Y^o}L_{R^i_!u(F)|y}\right)^{(-1)^i} =\prod_i\bigl(L_{R^i_!u(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i} \]
by definition of the function \(L\) on \(Y\).

blocks.title: Corollary.
blocks.html:
Corollary.

Let \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a finite morphism of schemes of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\), and \(F\) a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf on \(X\). Property \((P_{F,u})\) is true.

The lemma allows us to reduce to the case where \(Y\) is punctual: \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_q)\). Then \(X\) is finite and \(u_*(F)\simeq\bigoplus_{x\in X}u_*(F_x)\); to establish the formula \((P_{F,u})\), \(L_F=L_{u_*(F)}\), we are thus reduced to the case where \(X\) is itself punctual: \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_{q'})\). Let \(g:X\longrightarrow e\) and \(h:Y\longrightarrow e\) be the structural morphisms; by definition (n° 1) we have: \(L_F=L_{g_*(F)}=L_{h_*u_*(F)}=L_{u_*(F)}\), since \(g=h\circ u\).

blocks.title: Lemma 2.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.

Let \(u:X\longrightarrow Y\), \(v:Y\longrightarrow Z\), be morphisms of schemes of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\) and let \(F\) be a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf on \(X\). If the properties \((P_{F,u})\) and \((P_{R^i_!u(F),v})\) for every (i) are true, then \((P_{F,v\circ u})\) is true.
Indeed

\[ \begin{aligned} L_F &=\prod_i\bigl(L_{R^i_!u(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i}\\ &=\prod_i\left(\prod_j \bigl(L_{R^j_!v(R^i_!u(F))}\bigr)^{(-1)^j}\right)^{(-1)^i}\\ &=\prod_{i,j}\bigl(L_{R^j_!v(R^i_!u(F))}\bigr)^{(-1)^{i+j}} \qquad\text{by }(P_{F,u})\text{ and }(P_{R^i_!u(F),v}). \end{aligned} \]
The Leray spectral sequence \(R^j_!v(R^i_!u(F))\Longrightarrow R^k_!(v\circ u)(F)\) and the multiplicativity of \(L_F\) with respect to \(F\) (n° 1, Proposition 1 a)) finally give
\[ L_F=\prod_k\bigl(L_{R^k_!(v\circ u)(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^k}, \]
which is what we wanted to prove.

blocks.title: Lemma 3.
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.

Let \(X\) be a scheme of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\), and let \(g:X\longrightarrow e\) be its structural morphism.

a) Consider a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), a closed subscheme \(Y\) of \(X\) and the complementary open \(U=X-Y\). If the theorem is true for two of the pairs \((X,F)\), \((Y,F|Y)\), \((U,F|U)\), it is true for the third.

b) Consider an exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow F' \longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F''\longrightarrow0\) of constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaves on \(X\); if the theorem is true for two of the pairs \((X,F')\), \((X,F)\), \((X,F'')\), it is true for the third.
We shall use only assertion a), but we also give b) for the sake of symmetry. The proofs of a) and b) are analogous; let us prove a) for example. The exact sequence
\[ \cdots\longrightarrow R^i_!g_U(F|U)\longrightarrow R^i_!g(F) \longrightarrow R^i_!g_Y(F|Y)\longrightarrow R^{i+1}_!g_U(F|U) \longrightarrow\cdots \]
and Proposition 1 a) (n° 1) give:
\[ \prod_i\bigl(L_{R^i_!g(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i} =\prod_i\left(L_{R^i_!g_U(F|U)}L_{R^i_!g_Y(F|Y)}\right)^{(-1)^i} \]
and Proposition 1 b) (n° 1) then allows us to conclude.
We may summarize the arguments of Lemmas 1, 2 and 3 by saying that, by Proposition 1 of n° 1, the function \(L_F\) has formal properties analogous to those of cohomology with proper supports.
Let us now use the preceding lemmas to reduce to the case where \(X\) is the projective line.

1st Reduction. by noetherian induction we can construct a partition \((U_j)_{1\leq j\leq r}\) of \(X\) by affine schemes such that \(U_{j+1}\) is open in \(X-\bigcup_{i=1}^jU_i\) for \(j=1,\ldots,r-1\). Suppose the theorem established for every pair \((U,G)\) of an affine scheme \(U\) of finite type over \(\mathbb F_p\) and a constructible \(\Omega\)-sheaf \(G\) on \(U\); it is then true for \((U_j,F|U_j)\), \(j=1,\ldots,r\); thanks to Lemma 3 a) we deduce by descending induction on \(j\) that it is true for
\[ \left(\bigcup_{i\geq j}U_i,F\bigm|\bigcup_{i\geq j}U_i\right), \]
hence for \((X,F)\), since \(X=\bigcup_{i\geq1}U_i\).
It therefore suffices to prove the theorem in the case where \(X\) is affine.

2nd Reduction: suppose \(X\) affine, of dimension \(n\); there exists a finite morphism \(u:X\longrightarrow\mathbb E^n\) of \(X\) into the numerical space of dimension \(n\). Let us argue by induction on \(n\). For \(n=0\), \(X\) is finite over \(e\) and the theorem is true by the corollary of Lemma 1. If \(n\geq1\), suppose the theorem established for affine schemes of dimension \(n-1\); let \(u:X\longrightarrow\mathbb E^n\) be a finite morphism and \(v:\mathbb E^n\longrightarrow\mathbb E^1\) a projection morphism, defined by means of an isomorphism \(\mathbb E^n\simeq\mathbb E^1\times\mathbb E^{n-1}\); by composing we obtain a morphism \(w=v\circ u:X\longrightarrow\mathbb E^1\) whose fibers are affine schemes of dimension \(n-1\). Lemma 1 then shows that the property \((P_{F,w})\) is true by the induction hypothesis. Thanks to Lemma 2 we see that it suffices to establish the theorem for the pairs
\((\mathbb E^1,R^i_!w(F))\). We are thus reduced to the case where \(X=\mathbb E^1\) is the affine line.

3rd Reduction: suppose \(X=\mathbb E^1\); let \(i:\mathbb E^1\longrightarrow\mathbb P^1\) be the immersion of the affine line into the projective line \(\mathbb P^1\), and let \(Z=\mathbb P^1-i(\mathbb E^1)\) be the closed subscheme reduced to the point at infinity of \(\mathbb P^1\); since the theorem is true for \((Z,i_!(F)|Z)\), it amounts to the same, by Lemma 3 a), to establish it for \((\mathbb E^1,F)\) or for \((\mathbb P^1,i_!(F))\) (since (i) is an open immersion we have: \(F=i_!(F)|\mathbb E^1\)).
n° 3. Proof of the theorem.

At the end of these reductions, it thus remains to establish the theorem in the case where \(X\) is the projective line. We shall first transform formula (1) of the statement into a formula of Lefschetz type, involving traces; this last formula will be proved in the particular case where \(X\) is a projective and smooth curve.
Let us take up formula (1) again:
\[ L_F=\prod_i\bigl(L_{R^i_!g(F)}\bigr)^{(-1)^i} =\frac{P_F^1\cdots P_F^{2n-1}}{P_F^0\cdots P_F^{2n}}, \qquad P_F^i(t)=\det\bigl(1-f^{-1}_{H_c^i(\overline X,\overline F)}t\bigr). \]
The logarithm of the left-hand side can be written:
\[ \sum_{x\in X^o}\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \operatorname{Tr}(f_{F_x}^{-nd(x)})t^{nd(x)}/n =\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left(\sum_{d(x)\mid n}d(x)\operatorname{Tr}(f_{F_x}^{-n})\right)t^n/n \]
while that of the right-hand side is:
\[ \sum_{n\geq1}\left(\sum_i(-1)^i \operatorname{Tr}(f^{-n}_{H_c^i(\overline X,\overline F)})\right)t^n/n. \]
Comparing the coefficients of \(t^n/n\), we see that we must establish:
\[ (2)\qquad \sum_{d(x)\mid n}d(x)\operatorname{Tr}(f_{F_x}^{-n}) =\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}(f^{-n}_{H_c^i(\overline X,\overline F)}) \quad\text{for all }n\geq1\ ; \]
these computations are legitimate thanks to the fact that the field \(\Omega\) is of characteristic zero.

Let us transform formula (2) further by assuming that \(X\) is proper, which allows us to write ordinary cohomology instead of cohomology with proper supports. Recall that the category of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves on \(X\) is a category of fractions of that of \(\ell\)-adic sheaves; by abuse of notation, we shall henceforth denote by \(F=(F_\nu)_{\nu\in\mathbb N}\) the \(\ell\)-adic sheaf(*) on \(X\) which corresponds to the \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf underlying \(F\). Then \(\overline F=(\overline F_\nu)\) and the Frobenius morphisms \(F^*_{\overline F_\nu/X}\) define a morphism of \(\ell\)-adic sheaves
\[ F^*_{\overline F/X}:\operatorname{fr}_X^*(\overline F) \longrightarrow\overline F, \]

whence an \(\ell\)-adic correspondence on \((\overline X,\overline F)\). For each integer (\(\nu\)) we know that the endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}|R\Gamma(\overline X,\overline F_\nu)\) defined by \((\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X},F^*_{\overline F_\nu/X})\) is the inverse of \(f_{R\Gamma(\overline X,\overline F)}\) (§ 2, n° 3, corollary of Proposition 3); using the definition of \(\ell\)-adic cohomology, we then find that the endomorphism \(\operatorname{fr}_{H^i(\overline X,\overline F)}\) of \(H^i(\overline X,\overline F)\) defined by \((\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X},F^*_{\overline F/X})\) is the inverse of \(f_{H^i(\overline X,\overline F)}\); thus:
\[ f^{-n}_{H^i(\overline X,\overline F)} =\operatorname{fr}^{,n}_{H^i(\overline X,\overline F)} \quad\text{for all }n\geq1\text{ and all }i. \]
Let us apply this result in the particular case where \(X=x=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb F_q)\), \(q=p^d\), is reduced to a point whose degree \(d\) divides \(n\). We obtain:
\[ f^{-n}_{F_{\overline x}} =\operatorname{fr}^{,n}_{F_{\overline x}} =\left(F^*_{\overline F_{\overline x}/X}\right)^n \]
(\(\overline x\) = geometric point defined by an embedding of \(\mathbb F_q\) into \(\overline{\mathbb F}_p\)).

On the other hand \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}\) and (\(f_X\)) operate in inverse ways on the points of \(\overline X\). It follows that the projection \(\overline X\longrightarrow X\) establishes a bijective correspondence between the set of closed points of degree \(d\) of \(X\) and the set of orbits with \(d\) elements of \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}\) operating on \(\overline X\). Observing that the set \(\overline X^{\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}^n}\) of points of \(\overline X\) fixed under \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}^n\) is the union of the orbits of \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}\) whose cardinality divides \(n\), we can transcribe (2) in the « geometric » form:

\[ (2')\qquad \sum_{\overline x\in\overline X^{\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}^n}} \operatorname{Tr}(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline x}^n) =\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr} (\operatorname{fr}^{,n}_{H^i(\overline X,\overline F)}). \]
This is a formula of Lefschetz type on the scheme \(\overline X\) equipped with the sheaf \(\overline F\) and the correspondence \((\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X},F^*_{\overline F/X})\).

To lighten the notation, we shall from now on write \(X\), \(F\), \(h\) and \(h_F\) instead of \(\overline X\), \(\overline F\), \(\operatorname{fr}_{\overline X}^n\) and \((F^*_{\overline F/X})^n\) (since \(X\) and \(F\) will no longer occur, no confusion is to be feared); we shall assume that \(X\) is a projective and smooth curve over \(k=\overline{\mathbb F}_p\). The fixed points of \(h\) in \(X\) are all of multiplicity 1; indeed \(h\) operates by: \(T\longmapsto T^{p^n}\) on the completion of the local ring of a fixed point, \(T\) denoting a uniformizing parameter. The theorem will follow from the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition 2.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.

Let \(X\) be a projective and smooth curve over an algebraically closed field \(k\) of characteristic \(p\), \(F\) a constructible ℓ-adic sheaf on \(X_{\acute et}\) (with ℓ = a prime number ≠ \(p\)), \(h\) an endomorphism of \(X\) all of whose fixed points are of multiplicity 1, and \(h_F:h^*(F)\longrightarrow F\) a morphism of sheaves. The following formula, where \(h_{H^i(X,F)}\) denotes the endomorphism of \(H^i(X,F)\) defined by \((h,h_F)\), is true:

\[ (2'')\qquad \sum_{x\in X^h}\operatorname{Tr}(h_{F_x}) =\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}(h_{H^i(X,F)}). \]

In this Seminar an analogous Lefschetz formula was established for a constructible torsion sheaf \(F_ u\), annihilated by \(\ell^{ u+1}\), on the curve \(X\), equipped with a morphism \(h_{F_ u}:h^*(F_ u)\longrightarrow F_ u\). It reads:
\[ (3)\qquad \sum_{x\in X^h}\operatorname{Tr}(h_{(F_\nu)_x}) =\operatorname{Tr}^*\bigl(h_{R\Gamma_X(F_\nu)}\bigr) \]
where both sides belong to \(\mathbb Z/\ell^{\nu+1}\mathbb Z\).

Suppose that the ℓ-adic sheaf of the statement can be written \(F=(F_\nu)_{\nu\in\mathbb N}\), with \(F_\nu\) annihilated by \(\ell^{\nu+1}\). For each \(\nu\) we can write the formula \((3)_\nu\), and we see that the left-hand sides of these formulas, for variable (ν), are the components of an ℓ-adic integer which is precisely the left-hand side of the formula (2'') to be proved. It remains to establish that the right-hand sides of the formulas \((3)_\nu\) define, for variable (ν), an ℓ-adic integer equal to
\[ \sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}(h_{H^i(X,F)}), \qquad H^i(X,F)=\varprojlim_\nu H^i(R\Gamma_X(F_\nu)). \]
This follows from purely algebraic lemmas which we shall prove to conclude.

We consider a projective system \((K_ u)_{ u\in\mathbb N}\) of perfect complexes, with \(K_ u\in D_{\mathrm{parf}}(\mathbb Z/\ell^{ u+1}\mathbb Z)\), such that for each (ν) the transition morphism \(K_{ u+1}\longrightarrow K_ u\) is isomorphic in the sense of derived categories to the canonical morphism:
\[ K_{\nu+1}\longrightarrow K_{\nu+1}/\ell^{\nu+1}K_{\nu+1}. \]
We suppose each complex \(K_\nu\) equipped with an endomorphism
\[ h_\nu\in\operatorname{Fl}\bigl(D_{\mathrm{parf}}(\mathbb Z/\ell^{\nu+1}\mathbb Z)\bigr), \]

in such a way that \((h_ u)_{ u\in\mathbb N}\) is compatible with the transition morphisms, thus defining an endomorphism of the projective system \((K_ u)\). In the application to the proof of Proposition 2 we shall take \(K_ u=R\Gamma_X(F_ u)\) and \(h_ u=h_{R\Gamma_X(F_ u)}\); the condition on the transition morphisms follows via the « Künneth formula » from the analogous condition satisfied by the transition morphisms of the ℓ-adic projective system \((F_ u)\).

We propose to find a projective system \((K'_ u)_{ u\in\mathbb N}\), where \(K'_ u\) is a complex of free \((\mathbb Z/\ell^{ u+1}\mathbb Z)\)-modules of finite type, zero outside an interval of degrees ([a,b]) (independent of ν) and equipped with an endomorphism of complexes \(h'_ u:K'_ u\longrightarrow K'_ u\), in such a way that the following conditions are satisfied:
1) the transition morphism \(K'_{\nu+1}\longrightarrow K'_\nu\) is isomorphic (as a morphism of complexes) to
\[ K'_{\nu+1}\longrightarrow K'_{\nu+1}/\ell^{\nu+1}K'_{\nu+1}, \]
for all ν ∈ ℕ;
2) \((h'_\nu)_{\nu\in\mathbb N}\) is compatible with the transition morphisms.

3) \((K'_ u,h'_ u)\) is isomorphic to \((K_ u,h_ u)\) in \(D_{\mathrm{parf}}(\mathbb Z/\ell^{ u+1}\mathbb Z)\) for every ν, and these various isomorphisms can be chosen so as to be compatible with the transition morphisms of \((K'_ u)\) and of \((K_ u)\).
Suppose the construction of the systems \((K'_\nu)\) and \((h'_\nu)\) carried out; we can then write
\[ \operatorname{Tr}^*h_\nu=\operatorname{Tr}^*h'_\nu =\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}h_\nu^{\prime i} \]
for all ν ∈ ℕ. Let \(K'=\varprojlim_ u K'_ u\), and \(h'=\varprojlim_ u h'_ u\) (endo-

morphism of \(K'\)). We see that \((\operatorname{Tr}^*h_ u)_ u\) is an ℓ-adic integer equal to \(\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}h^{\prime i}\). Moreover the projective system \((K'_ u)\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition, and the same is true of the system \((H^{i-1}(K'_ u))_ u\), for every (i); it follows that
\[ H^i(K')\xrightarrow{\sim}\varprojlim_\nu H^i(K'_\nu) \quad\text{for all }i\qquad(\mathrm{EGA}\ 0_{\mathrm{III}}\ 13.2.3). \]

In the case of interest to us, where \(K_ u=R\Gamma_X(F_ u)\) and \(h_ u=h_{R\Gamma_X(F_ u)}\), we see that \(H^i(K')\) is identified with \(H^i(X,F)\), and that the endomorphism of \(H^i(K')\) defined by \(h'\) is identified with \(h_{H^i(X,F)}\). Consequently we can write
\[ \sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}h^{\prime i} =\sum_i(-1)^i\operatorname{Tr}h_{H^i(X,F)}, \]
and formula (2'') of the statement is thus proved.
The existence of the systems \((K'_\nu)\) and \((h'_\nu)\) rests on two lemmas, in which we consider a commutative ring \(A\), an ideal \(I\) of \(A\) and the functor
\[ T:L\longmapsto L\otimes_A A_0 \quad(L\text{ right-bounded complex of }A\text{-modules}), \qquad A_0=A/I. \]

blocks.title: Lemma 1.
blocks.html: Lemma 1. — Suppose the ideal \(I\) nilpotent. Consider a complex \(L\in D^-(A)\) which is flat in each degree, a complex \(M\in D^-(A_0)\) which is projective in each degree, and an isomorphism \(u:T(L)\xrightarrow{\sim}M\) in \(D^-(A_0)\). There exist a complex of \(A\)-modules \(L'\), bounded on the right and projective in each degree, an isomorphism \(v:L\xrightarrow{\sim}L'\) in \(D^-(A)\), and an isomorphism of complexes \(w:T(L')\xrightarrow{\sim}M\), such that \(u=w_0T(v)\) in \(D^-(A_0)\).
Remark. If \(M\) is assumed free (resp. of finite type) in each degree, the complex \(L'\) is also free (resp. of finite type) in each de-

gree. Indeed if a flat \(A\)-module \(N\) is such that \(T(N)\) is free (resp. of finite type), one shows easily that \(N\) is free (resp. of finite type) (Bourbaki, Alg. Comm., Chap. II, §3, prop. 5). Let us note the corollary: let \(L\in\operatorname{Ob}D^-(A)\) be such that \(L\otimes_A A_0\) is a pseudo-coherent resp. perfect complex of \(A_0\)-modules (i.e. isomorphic in \(D^-(A)\) to a complex bounded above, resp. bounded, and projective of finite type in each degree). Then \(L\) is a pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complex of \(A\)-modules.

blocks.title: Lemma 2.
blocks.html: Lemma 2. — Consider a complex of \(A\)-modules \(L\), bounded on the right, projective in each degree, equipped with an endomorphism φ, and an endomorphism φ₀ of \(M=T(L)\) coinciding with \(T(\varphi)\) in the sense of the derived category \(D^-(A_0)\). There exists an endomorphism φ' of \(L\) which coincides with φ in the sense of \(D^-(A)\) and satisfies: \(T(\varphi')=\varphi_0\).

Before proving these lemmas, let us indicate how they are applied to the proof of the existence of the systems \((K'_\nu)\) and \((h'_\nu)\). We argue by induction on ν, supposing \(K'_0,h'_0,\ldots,K'_{\nu-1},h'_{\nu-1}\) already constructed; we apply the lemmas with
\[ A=\mathbb Z/\ell^{\nu+1}\mathbb Z\quad\text{and}\quad I=\ell^\nu\mathbb Z/\ell^{\nu+1}\mathbb Z \quad(\text{so that }I^2=0),\qquad A_0=\mathbb Z/\ell^\nu\mathbb Z. \]

Let us first take \(L=K_ u\) and \(M=K'_{ u-1}\) in the first lemma. We obtain, taking into account Remark 2, the existence of \(K'_ u=L'\) with the transition morphism \(K'_ u\longrightarrow K'_{ u-1}\) given by \(w\), in such a way that condition 1) stated above is satisfied. The isomorphism \(v\) of \(D^-(A)\) allows us to transport \(h_ u\) to an endomorphism of \(K'_ u\) in the sense of \(D^-(A)\). We may assume that the latter comes from an endomorphism of complexes \(h''_ u:K'_ u\longrightarrow K'_ u\). Let us then apply the second lemma with \(L=K'_ u\),

φ = \(h''_ u\) and φ₀ = \(h'_{ u-1}\); we obtain the existence of \(h'_ u:K'_ u\longrightarrow K'_ u\) in such a way that condition 2) is satisfied; moreover condition 3) will be satisfied. Note that, with the notations of Lemma 1, if \(M^i=0\) for some index (i), we deduce \(L^{\prime i}=0\) thanks to Nakayama's lemma; the complexes \(K'_ u\) are thus indeed all zero outside a common interval of degrees.
To prove Lemmas 1 and 2 we shall need the following result:

blocks.title: Lemma 3.
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.

(i) Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be \(A\)-modules. If \(P\) is projective, for every \(A_0\)-linear map \(f_0:T(P)\longrightarrow T(Q)\) there exists an \(A\)-linear map \(f:P\longrightarrow Q\) such that \(T(f)=f_0\).

(ii) Suppose the ideal \(I\) nilpotent. For every projective \(A_0\)-module \(P_0\) there exists a projective \(A\)-module \(P\) such that \(T(P)\simeq P_0\). If moreover \(P'\) is a flat \(A\)-module such that \(T(P')\simeq P_0\), then we have \(P'\simeq P\).

Assertion (i) is clear, for if \(u_P\) (resp. \(u_Q\)) denotes the canonical map of \(P\) onto \(T(P)\) (resp. of \(Q\) onto \(T(Q)\)), the composite \(f_0\circ u_P\) is an \(A\)-linear map of \(P\) into \(T(Q)\), and \(u_Q\) is surjective; since \(P\) is projective, there exists a factorization of \(f_0\circ u_P\) through \(u_Q\): \(f_0\circ u_P=u_Q\circ f\) where \(f\) is an \(A\)-linear map of \(P\) into \(Q\); then \(T(f)=f_0\).

To prove (ii) we begin by observing that the case where \(P_0\) is free is obvious (without any hypothesis on the ideal \(I\)). We pass to the general case where \(P_0\) is projective by introducing a free \(A_0\)-

module \(L_0\) of which \(P_0\) is a direct factor and a free \(A\)-module \(L\) such that \(T(L)\simeq L_0\). The module \(P_0\) is identified with the image of a projector \(e_0:L_0\longrightarrow L_0\), and it is a matter of seeing that there exists a projector \(e:L\longrightarrow L\) lifting \(e_0\), which follows from Bourbaki, Alg. Comm., Chap. III, §4, n°6, lemme 2 applied to the sub-\(A\)-algebra \(B\) of \(\operatorname{End}(L)\) generated by an endomorphism of \(L\) which lifts \(e_0\) (whose existence is ensured by (i)) and to the (nilpotent) ideal which is the trace on \(B\) of the kernel of the canonical homomorphism \(\operatorname{End}(L)\longrightarrow\operatorname{End}(L_0)\). This proves the existence of \(P\) in (ii). Let moreover \(P'\) be as stated in (ii); then the composite isomorphism \(T(P)\simeq P_0\simeq T(P')\) comes, by virtue of (i), from a homomorphism \(f:P\longrightarrow P'\). Since \(P\) and \(P'\) are flat over \(A\) and \(T(f)\) is an isomorphism, we conclude that \(\operatorname{Gr}_I(f)\) is an isomorphism, hence \(f\) is an iso- morphism (\(I\) being nilpotent), whence (ii).

blocks.title: Corollary.
blocks.html:
Corollary.

Suppose the ideal \(I\) nilpotent. For every acyclic complex \(N_0\) of projective \(A_0\)-modules, bounded on the right, there exists an acyclic complex \(N\) of projective \(A\)-modules, bounded on the right, such that \(T(N)\simeq N_0\).
The complex \(N_0\), being acyclic, decomposes into short exact sequences
\[ 0\longrightarrow Z_0^i\longrightarrow N_0^i\longrightarrow Z_0^{i+1}\longrightarrow0 \]

where \(Z_0^i\) denotes, for each \(i\), the submodule of cycles of \(N_0^i\); moreover \(N_0^i=0\) for \(i\) large enough. By descending induction on \(i\), we see that the preceding exact sequences are split and that \(Z_0^i\) is projective for every \(i\). We lift \(N_0\) by lifting all
these exact sequences step by step, by descending induction on \(i\). It is thus a matter of showing that if
\[ S_0:\quad0\longrightarrow P_0\longrightarrow Q_0\longrightarrow R_0\longrightarrow0 \]
is an exact sequence of \(A_0\)-modules, with \(Q_0\) projective, and if \(R\) is an \(A\)-module such that \(T(R)\simeq R_0\), there exists an exact sequence
\[ S:\quad0\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow0 \]

of \(A\)-modules, with \(Q\) projective, such that \(T(S)\simeq S_0\). Now we can lift \(Q_0\) to a projective \(A\)-module \(Q\) by Lemma 3(ii), then \(Q_0\longrightarrow R_0\) to an \(A\)-linear map \(Q\longrightarrow R\) by Lemma 3(i). This map is surjective by Nakayama's lemma; we set \(P=\operatorname{Ker}(Q\longrightarrow R)\) to complete the exact sequence \(S\).

Proof of Lemma 2: since \(M=T(L)\) is projective in each degree, the hypothesis of the lemma amounts to saying that \(T(\varphi)\) is homotopic to \(\varphi_0\); thus there exists a homotopy operator \(k_0=(k_0^i)_i\), with \(k_0^i:M^i\longrightarrow M^{i-1}\) such that \(T(\varphi)-\varphi_0=d_0k_0+k_0d_0\), where \(d_0\) is the differential of \(M\). By Lemma 3(i), \(k_0^i\) lifts to a linear map \(k^i:L^i\longrightarrow L^{i-1}\). We thus obtain a homotopy operator \(k=(k^i)_i\) in \(L\). It suffices to set \(\varphi'=\varphi-kd-dk\) (\(d=\) the differential of \(L\)) to verify Lemma 2.
Proof of Lemma 1: we may (after replacing \(L\) if necessary by a quasi-isomorphic complex) suppose that the complex \(L\), hence also \(T(L)\),

is projective in each degree, which we shall assume in what follows; we may consequently suppose that the isomorphism \(u\) of \(D^-(A_0)\) co- mes from a quasi-isomorphism \(T(L)\longrightarrow M\) again denoted by \(u\). We shall first reduce to the case where \(u\) is surjective. The com- plex \(N_0\) defined by \(N_0^i=M^i\oplus M^{i-1}\), \(d^i(x,y)=(d^ix,x-d^{i-1}y)\) \((xin M^i), (yin M^{i-1})\) is visibly acyclic and projective in each degree; we define an epimorphism of complexes \(N_0\longrightarrow M\) by the projection \(N_0^i\longrightarrow M^i\) in degree \(i\). The corollary of Lemma 3 allows us to lift \(N_0\) to a complex \(N\) which is acyclic, bounded on the right and projective in each degree. We may then replace \(L\) by \(L\oplus N\) which is quasi-isomorphic to it (since \(N\) is acyclic) and projective in each degree; at the same time we replace \(u\) by the quasi-iso- morphism \(T(L\oplus N)=T(L)\oplus N_0\longrightarrow M\) defined by \(u\) on \(T(L)\) and by the epimorphism \(N_0\longrightarrow M\) on \(N_0\); it is clear that this last quasi-isomorphism is surjective.

So suppose that \(u:T(L)\longrightarrow M\) is a surjective quasi-isomor- phism, and let \(R_0\) be its kernel. The complex \(R_0\) is acyclic since \(u\) is a quasi-isomorphism, and it is projective in each degree, since the exact sequence: \(0\longrightarrow R_0\longrightarrow T(L)\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow0\) is split because \(M\) is projective in each degree. We can therefore, thanks to the corollary of Lemma 3, lift \(R_0\) to a complex of \(A\)-modules \(R\) bounded on the right, acyclic and projective in each degree. Let us now show that the morphism \(f_0:R_0\longrightarrow T(L)\) lifts to a mor- phism of complexes \(f:R\longrightarrow L\). For this we observe that \(R_0\) is homotopically trivial, hence \(f_0\) is homotopic to (0) and can be written \(f_0=h_0d_{R_0}+d_{T(L)}h_0\) where \(h_0=(h_0^i)_i\) with \(h_0^i:R_0^i\longrightarrow T(L)^{i-1}\). Lemma 3(i) allows us to lift \(h_0\) to \(h=(h^i)_i\), with \(h^i:R^i\longrightarrow L^{i-1}\),

and we see that \(f=hd_R+d_Lh\) is a morphism of complexes which lifts \(f_0\). A standard lemma \(comparer SGA 60/61, IV 5.7\) then implies that \(f\) is injective and that \(\operatorname{Coker}f=L'\) is flat in each degree. Since \(T(L')\simeq\operatorname{Coker}T(f)=\operatorname{Coker}f_0\simeq M\) is projective in each degree, it follows (Lemma 3(ii)) that the same holds for \(L'\). On the other hand, the canonical homomorphism \(L\longrightarrow L'\) is a quasi- isomorphism (being surjective with acyclic kernel \(R\)), hence we see that \(L'\) satisfies the conclusion envisaged in Lemma 1, q.e.d.

footnotes.html:
To simplify the notation,
we shall assume for the rest of the proof that
\(\Omega=\mathbb Q_\ell\). In the general case, one would have to replace
\(\mathbb Z_\ell\) by the normal closure of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) in
\(\Omega\). (Details left to the reader).
